segments.text: Charles Bradley, 1937. He gave a group of children Benzedrine to fix the headaches caused by spinal taps.

The headaches got worse. Their behavior in school got dramatically better. That accident produced an entire pharmaceutical category — methylphenidate, Ritalin, by 2024 prescribed to over six million American children and adults.

And a parallel category arrived behind it. Natural alternatives. Saffron, omega-3, neurofeedback, mindfulness, elimination diets — sold in supplement aisles, on TikTok, in functional-medicine clinics.
Most of them aren't supported by the evidence the marketing implies. Some are. The peer-reviewed paper trail tells a different story than the supplement aisle.
Let's start with the most striking head-to-head trial. Saffron versus methylphenidate.

Baziar and colleagues, 2019, Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology. Tehran University team. Fifty-four children with ADHD, ages six to seventeen, randomized to either methylphenidate at twenty to thirty milligrams a day, or to saffron at twenty to thirty milligrams a day. Six weeks.
And the result?

By week six, both groups showed comparable symptom reduction. No statistically significant difference between methylphenidate and saffron on either teacher or parent rating scales. Standardized Crocus sativus extract, the same the Tehran group has used for a decade across depression, OCD, and postmenopausal trials.
That's a small trial. What's the read?

Sample size of fifty-four. Six weeks. Single site. No sham. Replication is needed before saffron is considered an evidence-based alternative. But the finding is not isolated. It sits inside a saffron-RCT corpus across psychiatry that no one has demolished.
Move to omega-3. The meta-analysis number.

Bloch and Qawasmi, 2011, JAACAP. Pooled ten randomized trials, six hundred ninety-nine children. Combined effect size — standardized mean difference of zero point three one, p less than zero point zero zero zero one. Real, small, dose-related. Larger when EPA is the dominant fatty acid and at least five hundred milligrams a day.
Compared to methylphenidate's effect size?
Methylphenidate sits at zero point seven to one point zero in equivalent meta-analyses. Omega-3 moves the needle. It does not move it as far. It is best understood as an adjunct, not a replacement.
Neurofeedback. The most-marketed natural intervention. What does the evidence actually show?

This is where the literature splits sharply. Cortese and colleagues, 2016, JAACAP. Thirteen RCTs of neurofeedback for childhood ADHD. They ran two analyses. With non-blinded outcome measures — teacher and parent reports — neurofeedback showed a moderate effect.
And blinded?

When raters were blinded to treatment allocation, the effect collapsed toward zero. That is the technical signature of an intervention whose apparent efficacy depends on rater expectancy, not on changed neurophysiology.
Was that ever directly tested with a sham control?

Schönenberg and colleagues, 2017, Lancet Psychiatry. Real neurofeedback versus sham neurofeedback versus meta-cognitive group therapy. All three groups improved equivalently. The neurofeedback-specific effect was zero. The marketing market is roughly four hundred million dollars per year. It has not adjusted.
The ENIGMA brain-imaging study. What did it actually find?

Hoogman and the ENIGMA-ADHD Working Group, 2017, Lancet Psychiatry. Largest brain-imaging study ever in ADHD — seventeen hundred thirteen ADHD cases, fifteen hundred twenty-nine controls, twenty-three sites worldwide. People with ADHD have measurably smaller volumes in five subcortical structures: accumbens, amygdala, caudate, hippocampus, putamen. Plus smaller intracranial volume overall.
Effect sizes?

Small. Cohen's d in the zero point one zero to zero point two zero range. But consistent across the largest sample assembled in the field. ADHD has reproducible brain-volume signatures. That removes is-ADHD-real from honest discussion. It also constrains what kind of intervention can plausibly do something.
Walk through the INCA elimination diet trial. The Lancet one.

Pelsser and colleagues, 2011, Lancet. One hundred children with ADHD randomized to either a strict five-week few-foods diet — lamb, rice, pear, water, low-allergen vegetables — or to standard healthy-eating advice. In the diet group, sixty-four percent of children met responder criteria, forty percent or greater reduction on ADHD-RS. In the control group, zero percent.
That sounds too clean. Critique?

The diet is so restrictive that double-blinding is impossible. Some reviewers argue regression to the mean and parental expectancy account for most of the signal. Pelsser's group has published a second randomized trial replicating the effect. The defensible reading: in a subset of children — possibly with family history of food sensitivity, possibly not — dietary triggers contribute meaningfully to symptoms, and elimination identifies them.
Mindfulness, exercise, iron, magnesium, zinc — quick rankings.

Mindfulness — moderate effect on self-report measures, smaller on objective neurocognitive tasks. Cairncross and Miller 2020 meta-analysis showed pooled SMD around zero point six. Exercise — Cerrillo-Urbina 2015 meta showed effect sizes zero point four to zero point six six in aerobic and skill-based protocols. Iron — Konofal 2008 showed eleven-point ADHD-RS drop in non-anemic but ferritin-low children with twelve weeks of supplementation. Zinc — Bilici 2004 showed effect in low-baseline subgroups, weak in unselected.
And the pattern across all of these?

Real effects exist for several interventions. None match the medication's effect size at the population level. The interventions with strongest evidence — saffron, omega-3, dietary protocols — are also the ones least promoted on TikTok. The intervention with the largest market and weakest blinded evidence is neurofeedback.
Read the What we can say, what we can't section.

We can say: saffron at twenty to thirty milligrams matched methylphenidate symptom reduction in one peer-reviewed RCT. Omega-3 produces a real, small effect across ten or more trials. Neurofeedback's apparent efficacy collapses when raters are blinded. INCA elimination diet produced sixty-four percent responder rate. ADHD has documented structural brain differences at the population level.

We can't say: that any single natural intervention reliably matches methylphenidate's effect size across the general ADHD population. We can't say: that responders to saffron, dietary, or omega-3 protocols can be identified in advance. We can't say: that natural interventions are categorically safer than stimulants. Iron in non-deficient children carries risk. Saffron has uterine-stimulant effects relevant to pregnancy.
Practical takeaway. Listener has a child. Or has been told they have ADHD. Methylphenidate is not on the table for whatever reason. Where do they start?

Strongest single-study head-to-head: saffron. Strongest meta-analytic support: omega-3 EPA-dominant at five hundred milligrams or more. Highest responder rate in motivated families: supervised elimination diet. Largest market with weakest blinded evidence: neurofeedback. Most of what's sold as natural ADHD remedy has none of this trial backing. The brand on the bottle is not the evidence base.
The article ends with a question. The honest map exists. The question is whether the patient or the parent gets shown the map — or just the marketing.

If this is the question that interests you, the related transmission worth reading next is the cognitive-science breakdown of how to stop overthinking — same pillar, same investigator-grade methodology, different intervention class.
